title: Retention Check: Linux Security — Permissions and Filesystem

questions.q: What does the SUID bit do when set on a root-owned executable?
questions.a: The process runs with root's effective UID regardless of who launches it. Any user who executes the binary gets root's privilege level for the duration of that process execution.

questions.q: Write the bash command to find all SUID binaries on a Linux system.
questions.a: `find / -perm -4000 -type f 2>/dev/null` — finds all files with the SUID bit set, suppressing permission errors from directories the current user cannot read.

questions.q: Name the four standard Linux persistence locations for cron and startup mechanisms.
questions.a: /etc/cron.d/ (system cron jobs), /var/spool/cron/crontabs/ (user crontabs), ~/.config/systemd/user/ (user systemd services), /etc/rc.local (legacy startup script).

questions.q: Why does the Filesystem Hierarchy Standard (FHS) aid detection even without specific monitoring rules?
questions.a: FHS defines canonical locations for each type of file and service. Legitimate services run from /usr/bin, /opt, /usr/local. Any persistent process running from /tmp, /dev/shm, or user home directories violates FHS norms and is immediately anomalous — the predictability of legitimate software is the detection signal.
